title: Staff+ Project Selection & Scoping
description: How Staff+ engineers pick high-impact projects, scope work at the right level, navigate ambiguity, and say no to low-leverage work. Covers the ICE framework, opportunity cost thinking, the 80/20 rule applied to Staff+ scope, stakeholder alignment, and real SAP examples where choosing the right project (or declining the wrong one) defined career trajectory.

sections.title: The ICE Framework — Impact, Confidence, Ease

description.value:
Staff+ engineers are evaluated on impact, not activity. The ICE framework provides a structured way to evaluate project candidates before committing engineering resources.

Impact: How much does this project move the business metrics? Measured in: revenue protected/generated, engineering hours saved, latency reduced, incident reduction. A Staff+ engineer should estimate impact in business terms, not technical terms ('this will save 500 engineering hours/year' not 'this will upgrade the logging framework').

Confidence: How sure are we that the estimated impact is real? A project with high potential impact but low confidence (unvalidated assumptions) should be treated differently from a project with moderate impact but high confidence (proven pattern). High-confidence, high-impact projects are 'no-brainers.' Low-confidence projects need a spike/prototype first.

Ease: How difficult is the project relative to the team's capabilities? Ease considers: technical complexity, organizational dependencies, stakeholder alignment, and team readiness. A high-impact project that requires 10 cross-team dependencies may be harder than a moderate-impact project that is fully within the team's control.

Applying ICE at Staff+ level: A Staff+ engineer has the credibility to tackle lower-confidence, higher-uncertainty projects because they can navigate ambiguity. The ICE score for a Staff+ engineer should skew: impact (0.5 weight), confidence (0.2), ease (0.3). Junior engineers should reverse: impact (0.3), confidence (0.4), ease (0.3).

SAP Bridge: SAP project selection often follows business priorities set by the process owner (functional lead). The technical team implements. At FAANG, Staff+ engineers co-create the project selection with product management. The ICE framework gives the SAP engineer a structured way to evaluate project candidates — a skill that is often underdeveloped in SAP consulting because the 'what to build' decision is made by the functional team, not the technical team.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: ICE Score Tradeoff: High-Impact Low-Confidence vs Moderate-Impact High-Confidence

scenarios.tradeOff: +Potential for outsized career impact but -30-50% chance the project fails to deliver expected value
scenarios.context: You are proposing a fundamentally new architecture for the payment system. If it works, it saves $10M/year. If it fails, you spent 6 months and have nothing to show. At Staff+ level, the expectation is to take calculated risks like this — but only if you can articulate the risk mitigation plan.
scenarios.recommendation: Proceed with a 2-week spike to increase confidence before committing. 'I will spend 2 weeks building a prototype and validating the key assumptions. At the end of 2 weeks, I will present a go/no-go recommendation with updated confidence scores.'

scenarios.tradeOff: +High probability of success and quick delivery but -Lower career visibility and modest business impact
scenarios.context: You are proposing incremental improvements to an existing system: adding monitoring, fixing a known bottleneck, optimizing a database query. The impact is real and measurable but not transformative. This is safe, steady work.
scenarios.recommendation: Take this project for a junior engineer to own. You provide architectural guidance and code review. This frees you to work on higher-uncertainty, higher-impact projects. Delegation is the Staff+ multiplier.

description.value:
Staff+ engineers are given ambiguous problems, not well-defined tasks. The skill is in scoping the problem before solving it — defining the boundaries, constraints, and success criteria.

The scoping conversation: When a VP says 'Fix the payment system reliability,' the Staff+ engineer does not start coding. They: (1) Define the problem boundary — 'Which payment flows? Credit cards only? International payments too? What about refunds?' (2) Define the current state — 'What is the current P99 latency? Error rate? How many incidents per quarter? What is the P0 incident count in the last 6 months?' (3) Define 'fixed' — 'What are the success criteria? P99 latency < 200ms? Error rate < 0.01%? Zero P0 incidents for 3 months?' (4) Identify constraints — 'Are there any dependencies on the accounting team? Do we need to maintain backward compatibility with the mobile app v5.0?' (5) Propose scope — 'I'll start with the credit card flow, which represents 70% of payment volume. I'll work with the accounting team on reconciliation. I'll aim for P99 < 200ms by end of quarter.'

The 'two-week spike' pattern: For highly ambiguous projects, the Staff+ engineer proposes a two-week spike to reduce uncertainty: 'I'll spend two weeks analyzing payment logs, talking to stakeholders, and running experiments. At the end of two weeks, I'll present the problem scope, proposed solution, and effort estimate.' This converts an un-scoped request into a scoped project without committing to a specific solution upfront.

SAP Bridge: In SAP, scope is defined by the blueprint phase of an implementation project. The technical team receives a signed-off functional specification. At FAANG, there is no blueprint phase — you create the specification yourself through spikes, prototypes, and iterative alignment. SAP engineers who learn the spike pattern have a significant advantage: they know how to build enterprise-grade solutions, and the spike pattern gives them the missing skill of defining what to build.

Staff+ engineers must decline projects that don't meet the impact bar. Saying no is harder than saying yes — it requires confidence, data, and organizational trust.

How to say no at Staff+ level: (1) Show you understand the request — 'I understand we need to fix the payment system. Here's what I've learned from the last 3 incidents.' (2) Show the impact of saying yes — 'If I take this on, I will need to deprioritize the fraud detection initiative, which has an estimated $2M annual impact.' (3) Propose an alternative — 'What if the payment team handles this with support from an SRE for 2 weeks? I'll review the architecture and ensure the solution is sound without being the primary owner.' (4) If you must say no — 'I cannot take this on. Here are three other engineers who could. I'll mentor them through the architectural design.'

The 'yes if' pattern: Instead of 'no,' use 'yes if': 'Yes, I can lead the payment reliability project, if we: (a) get the accounting team to commit to the reconciliation API timeline, (b) defer the in-app messaging project to Q3, and (c) add one more engineer to the payment squad.' This shows you are willing to help but require conditions that make the project feasible.

SAP Bridge: In SAP consulting, saying no is seen as unhelpful. The expectation is to deliver whatever the client requests, within reason. At FAANG, saying no to low-impact work is a positive signal — it shows you understand leverage and can prioritize. The SAP engineer must unlearn the 'always say yes' consulting mindset and learn to evaluate requests on impact, not authority.

Every project you say yes to is a project you say no to implicitly. Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative you forego. Staff+ engineers think in opportunity costs, not project budgets. The question is not 'Can we do this project?' — it's 'What is the best use of our engineering capacity right now?'

Opportunity cost framework: (1) List all potential projects. (2) Estimate the impact of each in business terms. (3) Estimate the engineering effort for each. (4) Calculate the impact-per-engineer-month for each. (5) Rank by impact-per-engineer-month. (6) Pick the top projects until engineering capacity is exhausted. This is simple in theory but hard in practice because: (a) impact estimates are uncertain, (b) effort estimates are often wrong, and (c) stakeholders have strong opinions about 'their' projects.

The Staff+ role in opportunity cost: The Staff+ engineer facilitates the opportunity cost conversation. They do not make the final decision alone — product management and leadership are involved. But the Staff+ engineer provides the data, the framework, and the technical judgment that enables better decisions. When a product manager says 'We need to build X,' the Staff+ engineer responds with 'Here is what we would not build if we build X. Is that tradeoff acceptable?'

Visualizing opportunity cost with the 2x2 matrix: Plot projects on a grid: X-axis = effort (low to high), Y-axis = impact (low to high). The sweet spot is the top-left quadrant (high impact, low effort). These are quick wins. The trap is the bottom-right quadrant (low impact, high effort) — these projects consume resources without delivering proportional value. The Staff+ engineer's job is to kill bottom-right projects before they start.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Opportunity Cost: Short-Term Feature vs Long-Term Platform Investment

scenarios.choice: A: Ship the customer-requested feature in 2 sprints (high visibility, lower long-term leverage)
scenarios.tradeOff: +Immediate stakeholder satisfaction and PM goodwill but -Missed opportunity to build the platform capability that would serve 10+ features over the next 2 years
scenarios.context: A major customer needs a custom reporting dashboard. Estimated: 4 weeks. The alternative: build a generic reporting framework that allows customers to define their own dashboards. Estimated: 12 weeks. The PM wants the quick win. The Staff+ engineer sees the platform opportunity.
scenarios.recommendation: Propose a split approach: deliver a minimal version of the custom dashboard in 2 weeks (enough to satisfy the customer) and start the platform framework in parallel. 'We will give the customer something usable in 2 weeks while we build the foundation for the next 10 customers.'

scenarios.choice: B: Build the platform framework (lower immediate visibility, 10x leverage over 2 years)
scenarios.tradeOff: +Enables self-service for all future customers but -First customer waits 12 weeks and the PM is unhappy for 2 quarters
scenarios.context: You know from experience that every customer asks for the same dashboard with minor variations. The platform approach saves engineering time across 10+ customers. But the first customer will be unhappy with a 12-week wait.
scenarios.recommendation: This is the right long-term choice. The Staff+ skill is managing the short-term unhappiness: explain the tradeoff to the PM in dollar terms, get the customer a temporary solution (manual report via the PM), and celebrate the platform when the next 3 customers onboard in days instead of weeks.

description.value:
High-impact projects require cross-team alignment. A technically sound project will fail if stakeholders are not aligned. Staff+ engineers invest heavily in stakeholder mapping and alignment before the project starts.

Stakeholder types: (1) Decision-makers — the people who approve the project and allocate resources. Usually a Director or VP. They care about: business impact, risk, timeline, and cost. (2) Supporters — the people who will help execute. They care about: technical soundness, feasibility, and working with you. (3) Blockers — the people who could prevent or slow the project. They care about: their own team's priorities, dependencies, and risk to their systems. (4) Influencers — the people whose opinions shape the decision. They care about: being heard, having their concerns addressed.

The alignment process: Week 1-2: One-on-one meetings with every stakeholder. Understand their priorities, concerns, and constraints. Don't pitch your solution yet — listen. Week 3: Share a draft problem statement and proposed approach. Ask for feedback. Week 4: Incorporate feedback and share a revised proposal. Week 5: Present to decision-makers with a clear ask. Week 6: Start execution with stakeholder commitments locked in.

The 'pre-wire' technique: Before any formal presentation, meet individually with each decision-maker. Present your proposal, answer their questions, and address their concerns. By the time the formal meeting happens, everyone has already been briefed and has had their input incorporated. The meeting is a formality to confirm alignment, not a place for surprises.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "This stakeholder alignment process sounds slow. At FAANG we move fast. Why can't I just build the right thing and ship it? If it's good, people will adopt it."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Building and shipping without alignment is faster in the short term but creates rework when stakeholders block the launch." (weak, sounds like an excuse for slowness)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "The build-and-ship approach works when you have full ownership of the system and no dependencies. At Staff+ level, your projects span 3+ teams. A team lead on another team who wasn't consulted can block your launch for 2 weeks by asking 'How does this affect my latency budget?' or 'This new dependency breaks my deployment pipeline.' Two weeks of delay because of one unanticipated stakeholder concern — that's much slower than the 2 hours you would have spent on a pre-wire meeting. The alignment process is not about being slow. It's about avoiding the 10x slowdown that happens when you hit unanticipated resistance. Amazon calls this 'disagree and commit' — but you can't commit if you haven't disagreed first."
adversarialResponse.pattern: speed-vs-velocity — distinguish between 'fast in the first week' and 'fast to ship.' Stakeholder alignment is a velocity investment. It trades week-1 speed for month-3 velocity. At Staff+ level, velocity matters more than speed.

sections.title: SAP-to-FAANG Case Study: The Integration Hub Decision

description.value:
Background: Maria was an SAP integration architect at a large enterprise. She joined Google as an L5 engineer on the platform infrastructure team. In her first 6 months, she identified a critical problem: the team was maintaining 15 point-to-point integrations between services, each with different authentication, error handling, and monitoring patterns. Incidents were frequent and MTTR was high.

The ICE evaluation: Maria applied the ICE framework to three project candidates: (A) Build an internal integration hub — a centralized platform for service-to-service communication. Impact: 9/10 (would eliminate 80% of integration incidents). Confidence: 6/10 (complex cross-team effort). Ease: 3/10 (requires buy-in from 8 service teams). (B) Standardize integration patterns across existing services. Impact: 5/10 (reduces inconsistency but doesn't eliminate the fundamental problem). Confidence: 8/10 (straightforward documentation + linter). Ease: 7/10 (within team's control). (C) Build monitoring dashboards for the current integrations. Impact: 3/10 (improves visibility but doesn't fix anything). Confidence: 9/10. Ease: 9/10.

The choice: Maria chose project A (the integration hub). She used the 'two-week spike' pattern to build a prototype and validate the key assumptions. She pre-wired the 8 service team leads in individual meetings over 3 weeks. She addressed each team's concerns: 'Your authentication is different? The hub will support multiple auth providers. Your latency requirements are strict? The hub adds < 2ms overhead — here's the benchmark data.' She presented the proposal at the engineering review with pre-wired support from all 8 teams. The project was approved and delivered in 4 months. Integration incidents dropped by 85%.

Why this worked: Maria used her SAP integration expertise (she had built similar hubs at the enterprise level) combined with FAANG project selection skills (ICE framework, stakeholder pre-wiring, two-week spike). Her SAP background was not a liability — it was the source of her conviction that the integration hub was the right approach. The difference was she now had the framework to sell it and the organizational skills to align stakeholders.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Project selection is a core behavioral signal at L6+. The question 'Tell me about a project you chose to work on' tests whether you understand impact-driven prioritization. Use the ICE framework implicitly: 'I evaluated three projects. Project A had the highest potential impact but required cross-team coordination that would take 3 months to align. Project B had moderate impact but was fully within my team's control and could ship in 6 weeks. I chose Project B because the confidence was higher and we needed a quick win.' Also expect: 'Tell me about a time you said no to a project.' This tests whether you can prioritize and push back on low-impact work professionally.
